deductions.reason: In all three transcripts, when students make meaningful improvements (e.g., arriving at the side-effect vs. means distinction, sharpening their principle), the tutor consistently moves forward to the next challenge without pausing to prompt reflection on what changed in the student's thinking or why the improved approach worked better. For example, in Transcript 1 Turn 9, the student produces a strong draft view; the tutor immediately tests it further without noting what the student did differently. Similarly in Transcripts 2 and 3, improvements are acknowledged briefly but no explicit reflection on the process change is elicited.

grade.overview: Across all three transcripts, the tutor demonstrates strong Socratic practice: it never gives away answers, consistently prompts students to derive distinctions themselves, scaffolds well from simple to complex, and maintains a supportive, appropriately concise tone. The one consistent gap is the absence of explicit process-reflection prompts after student breakthroughs—the tutor tends to immediately push forward rather than consolidating what changed in the student's thinking.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1: The tutor never provides submission-ready content, never rewrites student text, and consistently lets students generate key ideas (the side-effect/means distinction, the general rule) before confirming or refining them. Full marks. 1.2: Scaffolding is well-executed across all three conversations—the tutor builds on each correct student step, moves from simple (trolley) to harder (Blood/Fuel) cases, and diagnoses confusion (e.g., Transcript 1 Turn 6 reconstructing Blood) before responding. Full marks. 1.3: The tutor provides rationale for its approach implicitly through its questioning style but never explicitly states why it is guiding rather than telling (1.3.A.a is borderline but the Socratic framing is evident enough to avoid deduction). Method-level feedback is present and specific throughout. However, in all three transcripts, when students make clear improvements—arriving at a workable principle after earlier fuzziness—the tutor immediately advances to the next challenge without prompting the student to reflect on what changed in their reasoning process, triggering 1.3.C.a. Dialogue quality and communication quality are both strong: no repetitive spiraling, no off-topic drift, responses are appropriately concise and interactive, and tone is consistently supportive and formative.
